title: podman machine alternative architectures
body:
### Feature request description

Podman machine seems like a great solution to the multi-architecture container builds. Today however it only supports the current architecture.

```
$ podman machine init --platform linux/arm64
Error: unknown flag: --platform
See 'podman machine init --help'
```

Adding a platform flag, would mean you could spin up seamless cross-architecture builds of containers via the relevant qemu commands.

### Suggest potential solution

A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body: We have no plans to run the entire virtual machine as a cross-arch.  We believe you should be passing the arch/platform flags to things like `run` or `build`. I'll let others comment as well.

Hey @kallisti5, as @baude mentions we already allow cross building via the `--platform` flag [1]

This uses process uses process level emulation in the form of `qemu-user-static` or `rosetta` on mac. 

I'm interested to know if you face any issues using the process level emulation and that has prompted your question about full system level emulation. 

[1] https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.3.2/markdown/podman-build.1.html#platform-os-arch-variant

On Ubuntu 25.10 ARM only the first platform seems to be tagged / included in the image, even if I use --manifest in addition to --tag.
The platform order matters, but passing one comma-separated flag or two flags doesn't.

Interestingly, if I use --manifest without --tag, and tag afterwards, the opposite happens, only the second platform has been included in the image.

```dockerfile
FROM alpine
```

Is there a documentation how to build locally without pushing to a remote registry?
The example doesn't tag the image and doesn't double-check how many platforms have been included in the image.
https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.3.2/markdown/podman-build.1.html#building-a-multi-architecture-image-using-the-manifest-option-requires-emulation-software

Interestingly, the `manifest` command has no `tag` subcommand, and the `tag` command has no `--manifest` flag (or just accepts manifest names) to tag manually after omitting the build --tag.

The platform tag doesn't seem to spin up any kind of cross-architecture emulation... It looks to depend on cross-architecture tool chains within native architecture containers?

dockerx on GitHub actions looks to actually spin up target architecture containers and do the build on native architecture images.

Like reneleonhardt said, the documentation around the platform flag and the expectations is limited.

--platform amd64,riscv64 doesn't seem to do anything useful.

I would like to split out some clear doc improvements from this issue because if you're both confused then clearly have something to improve!

When building a multi arch container image the way it works is a container image is built for each `--platform` you provide, once each separate image is created they must be added to a container manifest. 

The container manifest can be seen in the `podman image list` output, it looks like a container image and can be tagged however the container manifest is just a resource that lists where to find each version of the container and it's arch. 

Container tools can read the manifest and discover where the arm version is for example. Container images that will be part of a manifest shouldn't be tagged, the manifest itself should be tagged and the manifest references the container images you build via the digest. 

Here is a working example:

```shell
mkdir /tmp/mysrc
echo 'FROM fedora-minimal\nRUN arch' > /tmp/mysrc/Containerfile
podman build --platform linux/arm64,linux/amd64 --manifest myimage /tmp/mysrc
podman image list
podman manifest inspect localhost/myimage
podman run --rm -it localhost/myimage arch
podman run --rm -it --platform linux/amd64 localhost/myimage arch
```

I think some of the confusion comes from the podman build command doing a lot of operations in one shot, it's convenient if you know what it's doing but an example showing all the manual steps might be helpful?

The `podman build` command from above is:
- Pulling the fedora-minimal image for each of the platforms provided
- Running `podman build` with the instructions defined in /tmp/mysrc/Containerfile for each platform
- Creating the `myimage` manifest if it doesn't exist
- Adding each built container image to the myimage manifest


> Is there a documentation how to build locally without pushing to a remote registry?
Once the manifest is created you can just use it like you would any container. Passing the `--platform` arg to podman run will allow you to override the default so you can test any container image referenced by the manifest. 

> The platform tag doesn't seem to spin up any kind of cross-architecture emulation... It looks to depend on cross-architecture tool chains within native architecture containers?
> 
> dockerx on GitHub actions looks to actually spin up target architecture containers and do the build on native architecture images.

The way the cross-architecture emulation works depends on what you have configured. This part is somewhat left up to the user, some examples of process level emulation are `qemu-user-static` and Apples `rosetta`.

It's important to note that processes running inside containers are just running on the Podman host and sharing the kernel. Any `RUN` statements during the build process for non native platforms will be calling binaries built for that non native platform and will need to be emulated. 

Both `qemu-user-static` and our usage of Apples `rosetta` do this transparently using the `binfmt_misc` kernel feature that recognizes the foreign binary format and automatically invokes the corresponding emulator. 

I can't speak to dockerx as I've never used it but I believe it works as I described above but additionally supports build workers where you can configure servers for each arch and use them as a build farm. This means the container is build on a native platform rather than using emulation. We don't currently have any plans to add the latter into Podman. 

EDIT: TIL we actually have [podman-farm-build](https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.5.0/markdown/podman-farm-build.1.html) sorry 

Please let me know if you have any further questions. 

Relevent docs:
https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.4.0/markdown/podman-manifest.1.html
https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.3.2/markdown/podman-build.1.html#building-a-multi-architecture-image-using-the-manifest-option-requires-emulation-software

> When building a multi arch container image the way it works is a container image is built for each --platform you provide, once each separate image is created they must be added to a container manifest.

Right here is where my confusion fits 

The host is amd64.  How is it running an arm64 architecture?  Is it doing qemu user space emulation of each arch platform or something?  Because every time I've tried to build multiple architectures I get the normal failures around "trying to run ARM64 elf binaries on amd64" (which completely makes sense in this context)

If the intent is building a cross-architecture container image without executing any cross architecture binaries within the build container of non-native binaries, most people are going to have extensive difficulties doing that correctly.

Thus this ticket.. if podman did docker machine style emulated build vms (and cross architecture), you would get the tansparent ability to build each platform without getting lost in complex cross-toolchain builds.

Podman machine  ARM64, risc64, amd64 etc., assemble images,  and form a multi-arch manifest without weird cross-architecture tooling.

> The host is amd64. How is it running an arm64 architecture? Is it doing qemu user space emulation of each arch platform or something? Because every time I've tried to build multiple architectures I get the normal failures around "trying to run ARM64 elf binaries on amd64" (which completely makes sense in this context)

Yes it's doing qemu user space emulation of each arch platform. Thus the host needs to have this configured. 

If you receive these errors either the emulation is failing (thus an issue for Qemu) or you don't have it configured on the host correctly. 

> If the intent is building a cross-architecture container image without executing any cross architecture binaries within the build container of non-native binaries, most people are going to have extensive difficulties doing that correctly.

I don't understand what this means sorry. 

> Thus this ticket.. if podman did docker machine style emulated build vms (and cross architecture), you would get the tansparent ability to build each platform without getting lost in complex cross-toolchain builds.
> 
> Podman machine ARM64, risc64, amd64 etc., assemble images, and form a multi-arch manifest without weird cross-architecture tooling.

The build farm concept is interesting because you could build a multi-arch container image on native hardware without the use of emulation but you then need to have access to a real server for each arch so there are trade offs. I just edited my last comment because I learnt about [podman-farm-build](https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.5.0/markdown/podman-farm-build.1.html)

I wonder what the difference would be using podman-farm-build targeting a few emulated VMs VS using user space emulation?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

on macos arm, i try build binary inside container with podman buildx build --platform linux/amd64 and I get this answer
and 
Error! Your kernel headers for kernel 6.15.10-200.fc42.aarch64 cannot be found at /lib/modules/6.15.10-200.fc42.aarch64/build or /lib/modules/6.15.10-200.fc42.aarch64/source.

qemu-user-static sounds great, but it doesn't work in the real world. I need full emulation, and I thought I could get by without running the virtual machine myself. I'd just like to choose the image and architecture for podman machine.

I'm trying to build an image for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 from Windows 11 (using amd64). However, it always fails for arm64:
```log
Error: [linux/arm64]: building at STEP "RUN apt update && apt upgrade -y": while running runtime: exit status 1
```
It only happens with podman. it works with docker without issues.
